comment.body:
When you look at ATM IV across different expiries, that’s the term structure.
When you take today’s ATM IV and compare it to the vol risk premium on the same strike from yesterday, that’s fixed strike IV

comment.body:
Fixed strike vol is the real fear index. Vol traders know this. Cem Karsan was the one who brought it to X.
It’s simple..you take today’s ATM IV and check what the same strike was trading at yesterday or a week ago. You can look at different expiries, but the key thing is that it’s the same strike, which is why it’s called fixed strike.
This shows how the vol profile has shifted. Alma’s chart on this is straight-up genius. Take a few minutes and study it

comment.body:
There’s three kinds of vol. One is realized or historical vol, which is just what already happened, calculated from past data. Then there’s implied vol, which is the vol risk premium in options, or like Alma says, a bet on future volatility. Because of risk/reward, it’s also directly a mirror of the supply/demand ratio. The third one is expected vol, calculated with quant methods, like that Monte Carlo thing Alma posted once, but you don’t need to bother with that.
Alma usually switches between talking about historical vol and implied vol, but historical vol has zero effect on option pricing. Implied vol does. Alma’s said it a bunch of times..if you were paying attention..that IV is a direct input in option pricing formulas. Market makers even call it vega.
So it’s pretty obvious that when she talks about vanna, she means implied vol, ‘cause, once again, historical vol has nothing to do with option pricing or hedging. It’s just the result of price moves.
comment.raw_body:
There’s three kinds of vol. One is realized or historical vol, which is just what already happened, calculated from past data. Then there’s implied vol, which is the vol risk premium in options, or like Alma says, a bet on future volatility. Because of risk/reward, it’s also directly a mirror of the supply/demand ratio. The third one is expected vol, calculated with quant methods, like that Monte Carlo thing Alma posted once, but you don’t need to bother with that.
Alma usually switches between talking about historical vol and implied vol, but historical vol has zero effect on option pricing. Implied vol does. Alma’s said it a bunch of times..if you were paying attention..that IV is a direct input in option pricing formulas. Market makers even call it vega.
So it’s pretty obvious that when she talks about vanna, she means implied vol, ‘cause, once again, historical vol has nothing to do with option pricing or hedging. It’s just the result of price moves.

comment.body:
But among vol traders, what we use even more is the fixed strike straddle. That’s just an ATM put + ATM call.
Same concept as fixed strike IV, but instead of IV, you’re looking at the price of the straddle for the expiry you care about.
Gives a way more complete picture of vol

comment.body:
Actually, Volsig was expecting a rangebound day if price stayed above 6100..not this kind of sell-off. Volsig isn’t a trader; sometimes he nails things, but his role is more about explaining the dynamics. He was a market maker, so he never had to trade directionally.
Alma, on the other hand, explicitly said that probability was distributing downward. For me, the settlement value was the key.
Her writings are complex,not for beginners,but if you take the time, they’re totally understandable

comment.body:
It's always on the vol smile. Look..let’s say today’s ATM strike is 100, and its IV is 20%. Yesterday, the market was at 90, and the 100 strike OTM call had an 18% IV.If the market moved up, that same contract..which was an OTM call yesterday at 18% IV..is now ATM at 20% IV. That means vol increased as we moved higher.
That’s all fixed strike vol is..
📌 Check today’s ATM strike IV
📌 Compare it to the same strike’s IV yesterday
